howItWorks.title: How the Audio to Lyric Video Converter Works

steps.title: Upload your audio
steps.description: Drop an MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A file (up to 100MB). A free account gives you starter credits, and no manual lyric typing is required. The converter works directly from your audio file, whether it is a polished master or a rough demo, with files up to 100MB supported.

steps.title: AI transcribes and syncs
steps.description: AI transcribes lyrics from the audio automatically and syncs every line to the beat — in 50+ languages. Click any line to edit. The lyrics follow the rhythm and phrasing of the actual song, not a generic subtitle timer.

steps.title: Preview and export
steps.description: Watch the full lyric video before publishing. Confirm every lyric lands on time and reads clearly, then export MP4 in 16:9 for YouTube, 9:16 for TikTok, Reels, and Shorts, or 1:1 for feed posts. 1080p export is available on the free path (watermarked), with watermark-free, higher-resolution Pro exports when you need them.

whyChoose.title: Why Convert Audio to Lyric Video on LyricVideoMaker

highlight.title: One audio file in, a finished lyric video out

cards.title: Built for finished songs and demos

contentSections.heading: What is an audio to lyric video converter?
contentSections.intro: An audio to lyric video converter takes a supported audio file and creates a synced lyric MP4 from it. The conversion is not just format changing: AI listens to the vocal, transcribes lyrics, places each line in time, generates visual scenes, and renders a video in the aspect ratio you choose.
bullets: Input is audio only: MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A up to 100MB.
It converts vocals into editable lyric lines and beat-synced timing.
Output is an MP4 with the original music preserved.
It differs from a file converter because the creative layer, text timing, and video layout are generated too.

contentSections.heading: audio to lyric video converter specs at a glance

rows: Accepted input
Audio upload only: MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A, up to 100MB per file.
Lyrics
AI transcribes vocals automatically in 50+ languages, then lets you edit every line before export.
Sync
Beat-aware line timing keeps lyrics readable and musical instead of simply dropping captions on a fixed grid.
Visuals
Storyboard planning and AI scene-image generation create backgrounds around the mood of the song.
Exports
MP4 with original audio in 16:9, 9:16, or 1:1 for YouTube, Shorts, TikTok, Reels, and feeds.
Start
Free credits are available at the start, so you can preview the workflow before spending more time on a release.

contentSections.heading: Audio formats and when to use them
contentSections.intro: The converter accepts common audio formats, but the source you choose can affect transcription confidence, upload size, and how easy it is to archive your project later.

rows: WAV
Best when you have a final master and want maximum vocal clarity.
FLAC
Good for high-quality archives without the larger size of WAV.
MP3
Fastest for demos, references, and everyday uploads when the bitrate is clean.
M4A/AAC
Common from phones and music apps; works well when the vocal is not buried.
OGG
Useful for open-source workflows or exports from certain audio tools.
Rough demo
Acceptable for drafts, but expect more lyric cleanup if the vocal is low or noisy.

contentSections.heading: Best practices for converting audio to a lyric video
contentSections.intro: Use these checks before you render, especially when the same project needs more than one platform format.
bullets: Use the final mix if possible; changing the audio later can shift lyric timing.
Avoid clipping and heavy noise reduction, because both can make words harder to transcribe.
Name the file clearly before upload so exported variants are easy to trace.
Check repeated choruses carefully; repeated words can be correct but still split awkwardly.
Preview the vertical version separately, since audio timing stays the same but line wrapping changes.
If the song has multiple languages, review those lines before rendering all aspect ratios.
Keep the original audio file with the project so future edits start from the same source.

contentSections.heading: Why audio-first conversion is more reliable
contentSections.intro: An audio-to-lyric-video converter turns an audio file into a synced lyric MP4. The choices that matter happen before you render — here are the principles worth keeping.

cards.title: Start from audio for reliable timing
cards.description: Converting straight from the audio file means line timing is driven by the song itself, not a hand-built timeline — which is why an audio-first path beats retyping and dragging.

cards.title: Lyrics are structure, not decoration
cards.description: The words are the structure the viewer follows, not a caption added last. Always transcribe first, align to the beat second, and design the scene third.

cards.title: AI does the heavy lifting, you keep the taste
cards.description: AI handles transcription, timing, and scene generation. Whether the style fits, a line break should change, or you need a vertical, square, or widescreen export stays your call.

cards.title: The scene serves the lyric
cards.description: Viewers should never choose between reading the line and watching the scene. Keep contrast strong, lines short, and motion restrained so the visuals support the words.

cards.title: Review in three passes
cards.description: Watch once for words to catch transcription errors, once for rhythm to catch lines that arrive early or late, and once for platform fit so it still reads in a phone feed.

cards.title: One source, every format
cards.description: A single project becomes a 16:9 full version, a 9:16 vertical, and a 1:1 square without retyping lyrics or rebuilding timing — which is what makes a release feel planned.

cards.title: Build it while the song is fresh
cards.description: Make the lyric video while the song is still in your ear: you will hear awkward line breaks faster, catch misheard words sooner, and judge where the energy should rise or stay quiet.

contentSections.heading: audio to lyric video converter checklist before export
contentSections.intro: A quick pre-export review catches most quality problems while they are still cheap to fix.

rows: Audio readiness
Accepted formats are MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, and M4A; keep the file under 100MB.
Language review
The system auto-detects 50+ languages, but bilingual hooks and names deserve a manual pass.
Timing review
Beat sync gives a strong baseline; nudge any line that lands early or late against the vocal.
Scene review
Generated images should match the mood without making text hard to read.
Export review
Check 16:9, 9:16, and 1:1 separately because readable text size changes by format.

items.question: Is converting audio to lyric video free?
items.answer: Yes. Converting audio to a lyric video is free — sign up for free credits and export a watermarked video, no subscription and no credit card needed.

items.question: What input and output formats does the converter support?
items.answer: You can upload MP3, WAV, FLAC, AAC, OGG, or M4A audio files up to 100MB. The finished lyric video exports as MP4 for YouTube, TikTok, Reels, Shorts, and square feed posts.

items.question: Do I need to type the lyrics myself?
items.answer: No. Upload your audio file and AI transcribes every lyric line automatically in 50+ languages. Click any line to fix a word before export.

items.question: Can I convert an MP3 to a TikTok lyric video?
items.answer: Yes. Upload the MP3, and export in 9:16 for TikTok, Reels, and Shorts. Use the full track or create a shorter clip from the part you want to publish.

items.question: How long can my audio file be?
items.answer: Audio files up to 15 minutes are supported, which covers most singles, demos, hooks, and social edits. For best results, upload a clear audio file at 128kbps or higher.

items.question: Can I use exported lyric videos commercially?
items.answer: Yes. Every MP4 you convert is yours to monetize and release commercially. Free exports include a watermark — export with paid credits for a watermark-free version. Just make sure you have the rights to the audio you upload.
